  hnsw_config: { m: 32, ef_construct: 256 }   // degree; build breadth
};

// At search time, trade latency for recall WITHOUT rebuilding:
await client.search('docs', {
  vector: queryVec,
  limit: 10,
  params: { hnsw_ef: 128 }   // efSearch: raise for recall, lower for speed
});
Interview soundbite
"HNSW is my default: a layered graph with ~O(log N) search, tuned by M, efConstruction, and per-query efSearch. IVF clusters and probes cells; PQ compresses vectors; IVF-PQ is the billion-scale, RAM-cheap combo. DiskANN when it won't fit in memory."

lessons.title: Vector database landscape
lessons.lede: Dedicated DBs vs SQL/search extensions vs libraries — and when each is the right call.
lessons.html:
Three categories

1 · Dedicated vector databases

Pinecone (managed, serverless), Milvus / Zilliz (OSS, billion-scale, GPU), Qdrant (Rust, great filtering), Weaviate (built-in modules/hybrid), Vespa (heavy-duty search + ranking), Chroma (dev-friendly, local-first). Purpose-built: distribution, filtering, hybrid, CRUD, replication.

use when: vectors are core, scale + features matteravoid when: a few thousand vectors beside existing SQL

2 · Extensions to existing stores

pgvector (Postgres), Elasticsearch / OpenSearch (HNSW + BM25), Redis (RediSearch vectors), MongoDB Atlas Vector Search, SQLite (sqlite-vec). Keep vectors next to your relational/document data — one system, transactional joins, no new ops surface.

use when: you already run Postgres/ES and scale is moderateavoid when: billions of vectors or extreme QPS

3 · Libraries (not databases)

FAISS (Meta), hnswlib, ScaNN (Google), Annoy (Spotify). Raw ANN indexes — blazing fast, but you handle persistence, updates, filtering, sharding, serving. Great for embedding inside an app or research.

use when: you want a fast in-process index and controlavoid when: you need CRUD, durability, and a server for free

Decision heuristic

SituationReach for
<1M vectors, already on Postgrespgvector (don't add a system)
Managed, no ops team, fast startPinecone / Zilliz Cloud
OSS, self-host, heavy filteringQdrant / Weaviate
Billion-scale, GPU, cost controlMilvus
Keyword + vector in one engine alreadyElasticsearch / OpenSearch / Vespa
In-process, embedded, researchFAISS / hnswlib
Gotcha: pgvector is not free of trade-offs
pgvector is fantastic operationally, but heavy vector workloads compete with your OLTP traffic for the same RAM/CPU, and its ANN (IVFFlat / HNSW) tops out below dedicated engines at very high scale. Great until it isn't — know your ceiling.
Interview soundbite
"I don't add a vector DB by default. Under ~1M vectors next to Postgres, I use pgvector. When vectors are core — heavy filtering, billions of rows, high QPS — I move to a dedicated engine like Qdrant, Milvus, or managed Pinecone. FAISS/hnswlib when I just need an in-process index."

lessons.title: Core vector DB features
lessons.lede: Metadata filtering, namespaces, CRUD, persistence, sharding, and consistency — the production surface.
lessons.html:
Beyond "find nearest" — what a real DB gives you

Metadata filtering (the one everyone underestimates)

Store structured fields alongside each vector (tenant_id, lang, date, doc_type) and constrain the search: "nearest vectors where tenant = 42 and date > 2024." Sounds trivial; it's the hardest thing a vector DB does well.

Pre- vs post-filtering: the classic interview question
Post-filter: ANN returns top-k, then drop rows failing the filter — fast, but you may return <k results (or zero) if the filter is selective. Pre-filter: restrict to the matching subset, then search — correct, but breaks the ANN graph/cell structure and can be slow. Good DBs (Qdrant, Weaviate) do filtered ANN with integrated payload indexes to get both. Naming this trade-off is a strong signal.

Namespaces / multi-tenancy

Partition one index into isolated namespaces (Pinecone) or collections/shards per tenant. Keeps tenants' data separated and queries scoped without a full index scan.

CRUD & upserts

Upsert (insert-or-update by id) is the core write. Deletes are often soft (tombstoned) in graph indexes and reclaimed on a rebuild/compaction. Update-heavy workloads stress ANN indexes far more than read-heavy ones.

Persistence & durability

The index lives in RAM for speed but must survive restarts: write-ahead logs, snapshots to disk/object storage, and replicas. A "library" like raw FAISS gives you none of this — you build it.

Scaling: sharding & replication

Shard (partition vectors across nodes for capacity/throughput) and replicate (copies for availability and read QPS). A query fans out to shards, each returns local top-k, and results are merged.

Consistency

Most vector DBs are eventually consistent on indexing: an upsert may not be searchable for a short window while the index absorbs it. Ask about "read-your-writes" if your UX needs a just-added doc to appear immediately.

FeatureWhy it matters in production
Filtered ANNReal queries are scoped by tenant/time/type; correctness + speed both required.
Hybrid searchDense misses exact terms; sparse misses semantics. Combine them (next lesson).
Quantization (SQ/PQ)Cut RAM 4×–32×; the main cost lever at scale.
Soft deletes / compactionDetermines update cost and index bloat over time.
Snapshots / replicasDurability + availability; RAM-only indexes are volatile.
Interview soundbite
"The hard part of a vector DB isn't nearest-neighbor — it's filtered nearest-neighbor, upserts against a graph index, durability of a RAM-resident structure, and sharding. I always ask pre- vs post-filter behavior and index consistency."

lessons.title: Hybrid search & reranking
lessons.lede: Why pure vector search loses, and the retrieve-then-rerank pattern that fixes precision.
lessons.html:
The failure mode of pure dense search

Embeddings are great at meaning but weak at exact tokens — a product SKU XT-4429, a rare surname, an error code, a legal citation. The embedding of a query mentioning XT-4429 sits near "similar-looking products," not the exact one. Keyword search (BM25) nails these but misses paraphrases. The fix is to use both.

Hybrid search = dense + sparse, fused

Run a dense (vector) search and a sparse (BM25 / SPLADE) search, then combine the ranked lists. Two common fusion methods:

MethodHowNote
RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion)score = Σ 1/(k + rank) across listsScore-agnostic, robust, no tuning of incompatible score scales. The go-to default.
Weighted sumα·dense + (1−α)·sparseNeeds score normalization; more tunable, more fragile.
Why RRF wins in practice
Dense cosine scores (~0–1) and BM25 scores (unbounded) live on different scales — summing them directly is nonsense. RRF only uses rank position, sidestepping the scale problem entirely. Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Weaviate, and Qdrant all ship RRF.

Reranking — the precision multiplier

First-stage retrieval (bi-encoder ANN) is tuned for recall: cast a wide net, grab the top 50–100 candidates cheaply. A cross-encoder reranker then reads each (query, candidate) pair together and produces a precise relevance score, reordering them. You feed only the reranked top-5 to the LLM.

Cheap wide recall first, expensive precise reranking on a small candidate set. Cross-encoders can't index — that's why they run second.

Named rerankers to drop: Cohere Rerank, Voyage rerank, BGE-reranker, Jina reranker, or an open cross-encoder like ms-marco-MiniLM. ColBERT is a middle ground — late-interaction, token-level matching that's more accurate than a bi-encoder and cheaper than a full cross-encoder.

Gotcha: rerankers add latency
A cross-encoder scores each pair with a full model forward pass. Reranking 100 candidates can add 50–300 ms. Rerank a small candidate set (top 50–100), not thousands, and cache aggressively.
Interview soundbite
"Pure dense search misses exact terms, so I run hybrid (dense + BM25) fused with RRF for robust recall, then rerank the top candidates with a cross-encoder for precision, and pass only the top few to the LLM. Recall stage cheap and wide; precision stage expensive and narrow."

lessons.title: Putting it together: retrieval for RAG
lessons.lede: How embeddings and vector DBs plug into Retrieval-Augmented Generation, and where quality leaks.
lessons.html:
The RAG loop in one breath

Ingest: chunk docs → embed chunks → upsert to vector DB with metadata. Query: embed the user question → ANN search (often hybrid) → rerank → stuff top-k chunks into the LLM prompt as context → generate a grounded, cited answer.

Retrieval is the R in RAG — the generator is only as good as the chunks it's handed.

Where RAG quality actually leaks

LeakSymptomFix
Bad chunkingRight doc retrieved, wrong fragmentStructural chunking, overlap, small-to-big
Query/doc mismatchShort questions vs long passages don't alignQuery expansion, HyDE, multi-query
Low recallRelevant chunk never retrievedHybrid search, raise k, tune efSearch
Low precisionContext full of noise, LLM distractedRerank, lower k after rerank
Stale indexAnswers from outdated docsIncremental upserts, TTL, freshness metadata

Query-side techniques worth naming

  • HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) — have the LLM draft a fake answer, embed that, and search with it; a hypothetical answer is closer to real answer passages than a terse question is.
  • Multi-query / query expansion — generate several paraphrases, retrieve for each, union the results.
  • Query routing — classify the query and send it to the right index/namespace.
War story: the "lost in the middle" trap
Cramming 20 chunks into the prompt can lower accuracy — LLMs attend best to the start and end of context and lose the middle. Retrieve broadly, but after reranking pass a small, high-precision set (often 3–5 chunks). More context is not better context.
Interview soundbite
"RAG is only as good as retrieval. I chunk well, retrieve with hybrid search, rerank to a tight top-3–5, and use HyDE or multi-query when short questions don't match long passages. Fewer, better chunks beat dumping everything into the prompt."

lessons.title: Measuring retrieval quality
lessons.lede: Recall@k, MRR, nDCG, and how to actually know your changes helped.
lessons.html:
You cannot tune what you cannot measure

"It feels better" is not an answer in an interview or a postmortem. Build a small golden set — queries paired with their known-relevant chunk ids — and score every change against it. A few hundred labeled queries beats endless eyeballing.

The metrics to name

MetricQuestion it answersCares about order?
Recall@kDid the relevant item appear in the top-k at all?No
Precision@kWhat fraction of the top-k are relevant?No
MRRHow high did the first relevant item rank? (1/rank)Yes (first hit)
nDCG@kAre the most relevant items ranked highest? (graded)Yes (full order)
Hit rateFraction of queries with any relevant hit in top-kNo
Which one to optimize?
For RAG, recall@k at the retrieval stage matters most — if the right chunk never gets retrieved, no reranker or LLM can save you. After reranking, nDCG / MRR tell you if the best chunk is at the top where the LLM will actually use it.

Two distinct evaluation layers

Retrieval eval

Is the right context retrieved and ranked well? Pure IR metrics above, computed against your golden set. Fast, deterministic, cheap.

End-to-end (answer) eval

Given the retrieved context, is the generated answer faithful (grounded, no hallucination) and relevant? Tools: RAGAS (faithfulness, answer/context relevancy), TruLens, LLM-as-judge.

Gotcha: recall@k has a hidden cost
You can always raise recall by increasing k — but every extra chunk costs LLM tokens and dilutes precision. Measure recall@k and the downstream answer quality; the best k is the smallest one that keeps recall high.

Also monitor in production

  • Latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) per stage: embed, ANN, rerank.
  • Recall drift — periodically compare ANN top-k against exact top-k on a sample.
  • Zero-result and low-score rate — a spike often means an ingest bug or a query-distribution shift.
  • Cost per query — embedding tokens + rerank calls + LLM tokens.
Interview soundbite
"I build a golden set and track recall@k for retrieval, nDCG/MRR for ranking, and faithfulness/relevancy (RAGAS or LLM-as-judge) end-to-end. Recall first — you can't rerank a chunk you never retrieved — then squeeze precision."

lessons.title: Indexing & ingestion at scale
lessons.lede: Batch embedding, incremental updates, re-indexing, build cost, and the dimension-mismatch trap.
lessons.html:
The ingest pipeline

Getting millions of documents into a vector DB is a data-engineering job, not a one-liner:

  1. Load & parse — PDFs, HTML, DB rows; extract clean text (strip boilerplate).
  2. Chunk — split with overlap and structure (see the chunking lesson).
  3. Embed in batches — send 64–256 chunks per call to amortize overhead; respect rate limits.
  4. Upsert — write vectors + metadata with stable ids (so re-runs update, not duplicate).
  5. Build/refresh index — HNSW graph or IVF centroids get built or updated.
Batching is the throughput lever
Embedding one chunk per HTTP call wastes 90% of your time on round-trips. Batch. For a 10M-chunk corpus, batching plus parallel workers is the difference between hours and days — and on APIs, the difference in your bill.

Incremental updates vs full re-index

Incremental upsertFull re-index
WhenNew/changed docs trickle inNew model, new dims, corrupt index
CostCheap per docRe-embed + rebuild everything
HNSWInsert into graph; deletes tombstonedClean graph, best recall
IVFAdd to cells; centroids may drift staleRetrain centroids on current data
IVF centroid drift
IVF learns centroids from a training sample. If your data distribution shifts a lot after that (new topics, new languages), the old centroids partition poorly and recall degrades. Periodically retrain on fresh data.

Idempotency & deletes

Use deterministic ids (e.g. a hash of source + chunk index) so a re-ingest updates in place instead of duplicating. For deletes, remember many indexes soft-delete (tombstone) and only reclaim space on compaction/rebuild — plan capacity accordingly.

The dimension-mismatch trap
Every vector in a collection must have the same dimensionality and come from the same model. Upserting a 1536-dim OpenAI vector into a 768-dim BGE index either errors or — worse — silently corrupts search. Pin the model + dims per collection and validate on write. 
// Batched embed + upsert with stable ids (pseudo, Pinecone-style)
for (const batch of chunkize(allChunks, 128)) {
  const vectors = await embed(batch.map(c => c.text));   // one API call per 128
  await index.upsert(batch.map((c, i) => ({
    id: hash(c.source + ':' + c.chunkIndex),             // deterministic = idempotent
    values: vectors[i],
    metadata: { source: c.source, page: c.page, ts: c.ts }
  })));
}
Interview soundbite
"Ingest = parse, chunk, batch-embed, upsert with deterministic ids, build index. Incremental upserts for the steady state; full re-index only for a model/dim change. Watch IVF centroid drift and never mix dims or models in one collection."

lessons.title: Production concerns & the migration problem
lessons.lede: Cost, memory, latency/recall tuning, quantization, multi-tenancy, monitoring, and re-embedding.
lessons.html:
Memory is usually the bill

HNSW keeps the graph and vectors in RAM. Rough math: N × dims × 4 bytes for float32, plus graph overhead (often another 1.5–2×). 100M vectors at 1536 dims ≈ ~600 GB just for the raw floats, before the graph. RAM, not CPU, is the scaling wall.

Quantization — the main cost lever

TechniqueCompressionCost
Scalar (SQ, int8)~4× smallerTiny recall loss; easy win, widely default.
Product (PQ)8×–32×+ smallerNoticeable recall loss; rerank exact top candidates to recover.
Binary32× smallerBig loss alone; great as a coarse first pass + rerank.

Pattern: search on the compressed vectors for speed/RAM, then rerank the top candidates against full-precision vectors to recover accuracy. This "quantize + rerank" combo is how you serve billions cheaply.

Latency/recall tuning knobs

  • HNSW: raise efSearch for recall, lower for latency — tunable per query, no rebuild.
  • IVF: raise nprobe for recall, lower for speed.
  • k: smaller candidate sets are faster but risk missing results before reranking.

Multi-tenancy

Namespaces/collections per tenant isolate data and scope queries. Watch the noisy-neighbor problem (one huge tenant starving others) and the small-tenant overhead problem (thousands of tiny indexes each with fixed cost). Some systems solve this with metadata-partitioned single indexes instead of per-tenant indexes.

Monitoring

  • Latency p50/p95/p99 per stage; QPS; error rate.
  • Recall drift vs an exact-search sample — the silent quality killer.
  • Index size, memory headroom, tombstone/bloat ratio.
  • Cost per query (embed + search + rerank + LLM).
The migration / re-embedding problem — the killer interview question
Vectors from different models (or model versions) are not comparable — they live in different spaces. To upgrade your embedding model you must re-embed the entire corpus and rebuild the index. For billions of vectors that's a large, expensive, carefully-orchestrated job. Mitigations: dual-write / shadow index the new embeddings, backfill in the background, then cut over atomically. Never mix spaces during the transition.
Gotcha: the query must use the same model as the corpus
If you re-embed docs with a new model but still embed queries with the old one (or vice versa), every result is garbage. Version the embedding model and pin query + corpus to the same version.
Interview soundbite
"RAM is the cost wall, so quantize (int8/PQ) and rerank against full precision. Tune efSearch/nprobe for the recall/latency point, monitor recall drift, and treat any embedding-model change as a full re-embed + rebuild — dual-write a shadow index and cut over atomically because vectors from different models don't share a space."

lessons.title: Interview cheat-sheet & rapid-fire Q&A
lessons.lede: Everything condensed into answers you can deliver under pressure.
lessons.html:
The 60-second summary

Embeddings map data to dense vectors where proximity = meaning. Vector DBs store them and do fast approximate nearest-neighbor search, trading a little recall for huge speed via HNSW/IVF/PQ. Real systems add filtering, hybrid search, reranking, quantization, and a re-embedding plan. Quality is chunking + retrieval, measured with recall@k and nDCG.

Rapid-fire Q&A

QuestionCrisp answer
What is an embedding?A dense float vector from an encoder where semantic similarity = geometric proximity.
Cosine vs dot vs L2?Cosine = angle (ignores magnitude); dot = angle+magnitude; L2 = distance. On normalized vectors, cosine = dot, and cosine order = L2 order.
Why normalize?So cosine collapses to a cheaper dot product (MIPS) and metrics agree on ordering.
Why not exact kNN?O(N×d) per query — hopeless at millions. ANN trades ~1–2% recall for 100×+ speed.
Why not kd-trees?Curse of dimensionality — spatial trees degrade to brute force above ~20 dims.
HNSW knobs?M (degree), efConstruction (build breadth), efSearch (query breadth, per-query tunable).
IVF knobs?nlist (cells), nprobe (cells searched); needs training; watch centroid drift.
What's PQ?Compress sub-vectors to codebook ids; 8×–32× smaller, lossy; IVF-PQ for billion-scale.
DiskANN?SSD-resident graph index for when vectors don't fit in RAM.
Recall@k?Fraction of the true top-k the ANN index actually returned. The core quality knob.
Pre vs post filter?Post = fast but may under-return; pre = correct but slow; good DBs do integrated filtered ANN.
Hybrid search?Dense + BM25 fused, usually via RRF (rank-based, scale-agnostic).
Bi- vs cross-encoder?Bi-encoder embeds independently (indexable, fast recall); cross-encoder scores pairs jointly (precise, rerank only).
Chunking default?Structural/recursive, ~256–512 tokens, ~15% overlap, rich metadata; small-to-big for context.
pgvector vs dedicated?pgvector under ~1M beside Postgres; dedicated (Qdrant/Milvus/Pinecone) when vectors are core.
Biggest scaling cost?RAM. Quantize (int8/PQ) + rerank against full precision.
Migration problem?New model = new space = re-embed whole corpus + rebuild; dual-write shadow index, cut over atomically.
Query/corpus model match?Must be the same model/version, or results are meaningless.
Lost in the middle?LLMs ignore mid-context; rerank to a tight top-3–5 rather than dumping many chunks.

Mnemonics

  • "Normalize → cosine is dot." The equivalence, in four words.
  • HNSW = "M, and two efs." Degree, build breadth, query breadth.
  • "Recall wide, rerank narrow." Cheap first stage, precise second stage.
  • "Different model = different universe." Never compare vectors across models.
  • "RAM is the wall; quantize to climb it."
If you remember one thing
Every design choice is a point on a recall ↔ latency ↔ memory ↔ cost trade surface. Great answers name the knob (efSearch, nprobe, quantization, k, chunk size) and the trade it makes.
Interview soundbite
"Meaning becomes geometry; ANN makes nearest-neighbor over that geometry fast by trading a little recall for a lot of speed. Everything else — hybrid, reranking, filtering, quantization, re-embedding — is managing the recall/latency/memory/cost trade-offs at scale."

quizzes.question: You L2-normalize every vector at ingest time. What becomes true about cosine similarity and dot product?

options.text: They now measure completely different things and must not be mixed
Cosine similarity equals the dot product, because each norm is 1
Dot product becomes unbounded while cosine stays in [-1, 1], so ranking differs

quizzes.explain: Cosine = (a·b)/(‖a‖‖b‖). When ‖a‖ = ‖b‖ = 1 the denominator is 1, so cosine collapses to the raw dot product — enabling fast inner-product (MIPS) search with cosine semantics.

quizzes.question: Why do classic spatial data structures like kd-trees fail for high-dimensional embedding search?

options.text: The curse of dimensionality makes them degenerate to O(N), no better than brute force above ~20 dims
They cannot store floating-point coordinates, only integers
They require the vectors to be L2-normalized first, which loses information

quizzes.explain: In high dimensions, distances concentrate and pruning stops working, so kd-trees/R-trees visit nearly all nodes — collapsing to brute force. That is why ANN uses graphs (HNSW), clustering (IVF), and quantization instead.

quizzes.question: Which HNSW parameter can you raise at query time to increase recall WITHOUT rebuilding the index?

options.text: M (the graph degree)
efConstruction (build-time candidate breadth)
efSearch (query-time candidate breadth)

quizzes.explain: M and efConstruction are fixed at build time. efSearch controls how many candidates are explored per query and can be tuned live to move along the recall/latency curve without rebuilding the graph.

quizzes.question: A billion-vector corpus won't fit in RAM and cost is critical. Which index approach fits best?

options.text: Flat (brute-force) for guaranteed 100% recall
IVF-PQ — coarse clustering plus product quantization for heavy compression
A pure HNSW graph with M=64 for maximum recall

quizzes.explain: IVF-PQ partitions the space and stores heavily compressed (quantized) vectors, cutting memory 8×–32×+, which is the standard billion-scale, RAM-limited, cost-sensitive choice. Flat and large-M HNSW are memory-hungry.

quizzes.question: In hybrid search, why is Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) usually preferred over a weighted sum of scores?

options.text: RRF is the only method Elasticsearch supports
RRF fuses by rank position, so it avoids combining dense and BM25 scores that live on incompatible scales
RRF guarantees 100% recall by searching every document exactly

quizzes.explain: Dense cosine scores (~0–1) and BM25 scores (unbounded) are not directly comparable. RRF uses only rank position (1/(k+rank)), sidestepping score-scale normalization, which makes it robust and tuning-free.

quizzes.question: What is the defining difference between a bi-encoder and a cross-encoder in retrieval?

options.text: A cross-encoder embeds query and document independently so both can be indexed
A bi-encoder embeds query and document independently (indexable); a cross-encoder scores the pair jointly (precise but not indexable)
They are the same thing; the names describe different vendors

quizzes.explain: Bi-encoders produce independent vectors, so document vectors are precomputed and indexed for fast recall. Cross-encoders feed query+document together for a precise relevance score but must run per pair at query time — hence the retrieve-then-rerank pattern.

quizzes.question: You upgrade from OpenAI text-embedding-3 (1536-dim) to a new model. What must you do before serving queries?

options.text: Nothing — modern embedding spaces are standardized and interoperable
Re-embed and rebuild the entire corpus with the new model, because vectors from different models are not comparable
Only re-embed the query; existing document vectors remain valid

quizzes.explain: Different models (or even versions) live in different vector spaces, so old and new vectors cannot be compared. You must re-embed the whole corpus and rebuild the index — typically via a dual-write shadow index with an atomic cutover.

quizzes.question: For measuring RAG retrieval quality, which metric matters MOST at the first (retrieval) stage?

options.text: Recall@k — if the relevant chunk is never retrieved, no reranker or LLM can recover it
BLEU score of the final generated answer
The raw cosine value of the top result

quizzes.explain: Retrieval is a funnel: nothing downstream can use a chunk that was never retrieved. So recall@k governs the ceiling on quality; nDCG/MRR then measure whether the best chunks are ranked highest after reranking.

quizzes.question: A vector DB does a POST-filter (ANN first, then apply a highly selective metadata filter). What is the main risk?

options.text: It may return fewer than k results, or even zero, because most top-k candidates get filtered out
It guarantees exact results but is always slower than exact kNN
It silently changes the distance metric from cosine to L2

quizzes.explain: Post-filtering runs ANN for top-k then discards non-matching rows. If the filter is very selective, few or none of the top-k survive, so you under-return. Pre-filtering or integrated filtered-ANN avoids this at the cost of complexity/speed.

quizzes.question: Retrieval quality is poor even though the correct document is clearly in your corpus. What is the most common root cause to check FIRST?

options.text: The embedding model is too small and must be replaced immediately
Chunking — chunks too large/small or splitting mid-idea so the right passage is never a clean match
The vector DB is using cosine instead of dot product

quizzes.explain: Engineers reflexively swap models, but poor chunking is the usual culprit: oversized chunks dilute the embedding, undersized ones lose context, and mid-idea splits prevent clean matches. Fix chunking before touching the model.

lessons.title: Course overview & the three axes
lessons.lede: Three ways to change what an LLM does — and the one-sentence mental model that keeps them straight forever.
lessons.html:
Every technique for steering a large language model reduces to changing one of three things. Interviewers love this question because it separates people who use LLMs from people who understand them.

The three axes are orthogonal — you can turn all three knobs at once on the same base model.

The one-liner that wins the room

Prompting tells the model what to do right now. RAG gives it facts it did not memorize. Fine-tuning rewrites how it behaves by default. Prompting and RAG live in the context window (runtime, weights frozen); fine-tuning lives in the weights (a training step, changed forever).

Runtime knobs (no training)

Prompting + RAG. Cheap to iterate, versionable in git, revertible in seconds. This is where you start 90% of the time.

fast iterationno GPUs

Weight knobs (training)

Fine-tuning. Slow to iterate, needs data + GPUs + eval harness, but bakes behavior in so you stop paying for it in every prompt.

needs dataneeds eval

What we'll cover

  • The knowledge vs behavior distinction (the single most-probed idea).
  • Prompting depth: in-context learning, structured outputs, prompt caching.
  • Long-context windows vs RAG — the 2024–2025 debate.
  • Fine-tuning internals: LoRA/QLoRA, SFT vs DPO vs continued pre-training, data, serving.
  • Cost models, combining approaches (RAFT, distillation), and a decision framework.
Interview soundbite

"Prompting changes instructions, RAG changes knowledge, fine-tuning changes behavior — the first two live in the context window with frozen weights, the third lives in the weights. Reach for the weight knob only after the runtime knobs plateau."

lessons.title: The core distinction interviewers probe
lessons.lede: The trap question: "just fine-tune it on our docs." Knowing why that is usually wrong is the whole interview.
lessons.html:
Here is the highest-signal misconception in the entire topic: people assume fine-tuning teaches facts. It mostly does not. Fine-tuning is great at teaching form, style, and behavior; it is a poor, expensive, and dangerous way to inject knowledge.

You want to change…Reach forWhy
Which facts the model can cite (docs, policies, tickets)RAGFresh, attributable, access-controlled, updatable without retraining
Output format / tone / persona / a fixed schemaFine-tuning (or a good system prompt)Behavior is exactly what gradient descent bakes in well
One-off task instructions for this requestPromptingZero cost to change, per-request flexibility
A skill the base model literally cannot do (new language, niche format)Fine-tuning + dataBehavior/skill acquisition, not fact lookup
War story: the "fine-tune on our docs" trap

A team fine-tuned a 7B model on 5,000 internal wiki pages to build a support bot. Result: fluent, confident, and wrong — it hallucinated plausible-sounding policy numbers, and every doc edit meant a retrain. They rebuilt it as RAG in a week and accuracy jumped, with citations. Facts change; behavior is stable. Put the thing that changes in the retrievable layer.

Why weights are a bad database

  • Lossy: facts get averaged into billions of parameters; the model interpolates and confabulates.
  • Stale: the moment training ends, the "database" is frozen. RAG is fresh on every query.
  • No provenance: you cannot show a source, redact a row, or enforce per-user ACLs on a weight.
  • Expensive to update: one changed fact ⇒ a full retrain + re-eval cycle.
Gotcha — the exception that proves the rule

Fine-tuning can teach facts, but you need massive redundancy (a fact repeated many ways, thousands of times) and you still lose freshness and citations. Continued pre-training on a domain corpus does inject knowledge — but it is the heaviest, most expensive tool in the box. Default to RAG for facts.

Interview soundbite

"RAG is for knowledge, fine-tuning is for behavior. Weights are a lossy, stale, un-citable database — so put facts in a retriever and use fine-tuning to shape how the model responds, not what it knows."

lessons.title: Prompting & in-context learning
lessons.lede: The cheapest, fastest knob — and how far it actually goes before you need anything heavier.
lessons.html:
Prompting is in-context learning: the model learns your task from the tokens in the context window at inference time, with zero weight updates. It is astonishingly capable and it is where you should always start.

The in-context ladder (climb it in order)

  1. Zero-shot: just ask. "Classify the sentiment."
  2. Few-shot: add 2–8 worked examples. Often the single biggest quality jump for the least effort.
  3. Chain-of-thought (CoT): "think step by step" — elicits reasoning for math/logic. Modern reasoning models (o-series, Claude with extended thinking) internalize this.
  4. System prompt: a persistent persona/role/rules block that frames every turn.
  5. Structured output: JSON mode / function-calling / constrained decoding to force a schema (OpenAI Structured Outputs, tool schemas, Outlines/Guidance grammars).
  6. Agentic patterns: ReAct (reason + act + observe), tool use, self-reflection loops.
The token tax (a.k.a. the forever cost)

Everything you stuff into the prompt — the system block, the 8 few-shot examples, the retrieved docs — is re-sent and re-billed on every single call. A 4,000-token system+examples preamble at scale is a permanent line item. This "forever tax" is the classic argument for baking behavior into weights via fine-tuning… but see the next callout.

Prompt caching changes the math

Anthropic and OpenAI now offer prompt caching: a stable prefix (your big system prompt + few-shot block) is cached server-side, so repeat calls pay a fraction (often ~10% for a cache read) and run faster. This quietly undercut the "you must fine-tune to escape the token tax" argument — often you can just cache the preamble.

Where prompting stops scaling

Prompting shines

prototypingtasks base model can already dofrequently-changing instructions

Prompting strains

very long, rigid formatshigh per-call volumelatency-sensitive with huge preambles
Interview soundbite

"Prompting is in-context learning — zero weight updates, iterate in seconds. Climb the ladder from zero-shot to few-shot to CoT to structured outputs. The classic downside is the per-call token tax, but prompt caching now makes a big stable preamble cheap, so exhaust prompting before you train anything."

lessons.title: Long context windows vs RAG
lessons.lede: Gemini has a 1M–2M token window. Does that kill RAG? The nuanced answer is a favorite senior-level probe.
lessons.html:
By 2024–2025, context windows exploded: GPT-4.1 and Claude at 200K+, Gemini 1.5/2.5 at 1M–2M tokens. A tempting take: "just paste the whole knowledge base in the prompt — RAG is dead." Interviewers want you to dismantle that.

Why long context does NOT replace RAG

DimensionStuff-it-all-in long contextRAG
Cost per queryPay for 1M tokens every call — brutal at scalePay for ~few K retrieved tokens
LatencyGrows with context length (prefill is expensive)Retrieval + small prompt = fast
FreshnessRe-paste to updateUpdate the index; instant
Access controlHard — all-or-nothing in the promptFilter by ACL at retrieval time
Accuracy at lengthLost in the middle / context rotOnly relevant chunks in view
Corpus size ceilingBounded by window (millions of tokens)Unbounded (billions, in a vector DB)
"Lost in the middle" & context rot

Empirical finding (Liu et al., 2023) and many follow-ups: models attend best to the start and end of a long context and degrade in the middle. Bigger windows ≠ uniform recall. A tight, reranked 4K-token RAG prompt often beats a 1M-token dump on precision — and costs 100× less.

The real synthesis

Long context and RAG are complementary. Modern systems use retrieval to fill a generous window: retrieve broadly, rerank, then hand the model a rich-but-relevant 20–50K token context instead of a stingy 2K one. Long context makes RAG better (bigger, higher-recall chunks), it does not delete it.

When long-context-only is fine

One-off analysis of a single big document (a 300-page contract, one codebase), low query volume, no freshness/ACL needs. Paste it and go — building a retriever would be over-engineering.

single doc, low volumelarge corpus, high volume, ACLs
Interview soundbite

"Big windows don't kill RAG — cost, latency, freshness, access control, and 'lost in the middle' all still favor retrieval at scale. Long context and RAG are complementary: retrieve + rerank to fill a generous window with relevant tokens, don't dump the whole corpus every call."

lessons.title: RAG in one lesson
lessons.lede: Retrieval-Augmented Generation: give the model an open-book exam instead of making it memorize.
lessons.html:
RAG = retrieve relevant chunks from an external store, then generate an answer grounded in them. The model takes an open-book exam: it does not need to have memorized your data, it just needs to read and synthesize what you hand it.

Embed → retrieve top-k → rerank → stuff into prompt → generate grounded answer (ideally with citations).

Why teams reach for RAG first

  • Freshness: update the index, not the weights. New policy at 9am is answerable at 9:01.
  • Citations: you can show sources — essential for trust, compliance, and debugging hallucinations.
  • Access control: filter retrieval by user/tenant/role so people only see what they may.
  • Scale: a vector DB holds billions of chunks; a context window holds thousands of tokens.
  • Cheap iteration: no GPUs, no training runs, no eval regressions from retraining.
Gotcha — RAG quality is retrieval quality

Garbage retrieval ⇒ garbage answer, confidently delivered. The hard parts are chunking, embedding choice, hybrid search (dense + BM25 keyword), and a reranker (Cohere Rerank, bge-reranker, cross-encoders). Most "RAG doesn't work" stories are actually "our retriever returns junk" stories.

Real tools: orchestration with LlamaIndex / LangChain; vector stores Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, pgvector, Milvus; embeddings from OpenAI, Cohere, or open bge/e5; rerankers from Cohere or open cross-encoders.

Interview soundbite

"RAG turns a closed-book exam into an open-book one: retrieve relevant chunks, ground the answer, cite sources. It wins on freshness, citations, access control, and scale — and its failure mode is almost always the retriever, not the LLM, so invest in chunking, hybrid search, and reranking."

lessons.title: Fine-tuning: full vs parameter-efficient
lessons.lede: Actually changing the weights — the difference between full fine-tuning and PEFT, and why almost nobody does full anymore.
lessons.html:
Fine-tuning continues training a pretrained model on your data so new behavior is baked into the weights. Unlike prompting/RAG, this is a real training step: gradients, optimizer, GPUs, checkpoints, evals.

Full fine-tuning vs PEFT

Full fine-tuningPEFT (e.g. LoRA)
What updatesAll weights (billions)Tiny added adapter (<1% of params)
GPU memoryHuge — weights + grads + optimizer states (Adam ≈ 3–4× weights)Small — only adapter grads/states
Output artifactA full model copy (13B → ~26GB fp16)A few MB adapter file
Catastrophic forgettingHigher riskLower (base is frozen)
Multi-task servingN full modelsN adapters, one base — hot-swappable
WhenBig domain shift, you own the base, lots of data + GPUsThe default for 95% of use cases
Rule of thumb

Start with LoRA/QLoRA. It gets you 90–99% of full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the memory, produces a tiny portable artifact, and lets you serve many adapters on one base. Reach for full fine-tuning only when PEFT demonstrably plateaus and you have the budget.

Toolchain: HuggingFace transformers + peft + trl, Axolotl and Unsloth (2× faster, less VRAM) for open models; the OpenAI / Google / Cohere fine-tuning APIs for hosted models where you just upload a JSONL and get an adapter back.

Gotcha — you can only fine-tune what you can access

You cannot fine-tune GPT-4-class frontier weights directly; you use the provider's fine-tuning API (limited to certain models). Full control (LoRA on any layer, continued pre-training) requires open-weight models: Llama, Mistral, Qwen, Gemma.

Interview soundbite

"Full fine-tuning updates every weight — huge memory, a full model copy, more forgetting. PEFT like LoRA freezes the base and trains a <1% adapter: near-equal quality, tiny artifact, many adapters per base. Default to LoRA/QLoRA and only go full when it plateaus."

lessons.title: LoRA & QLoRA, explained
lessons.lede: The low-rank trick that made fine-tuning fit on a single consumer GPU — with the exact math and hyperparameters interviewers ask for.
lessons.html:
LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is the insight that a fine-tuning weight update is low-rank — it lives in a tiny subspace. So instead of learning the full update matrix ΔW (d×d, millions of numbers), you learn two skinny matrices whose product approximates it.

The math (know this cold)

W_effective = W_frozen + ΔW
ΔW = B · A            # B is d×r, A is r×d, rank r is small (4–64)
h = W_frozen·x + (α / r) · (B · A · x)
  • W_frozen stays fixed — no gradients flow to it.
  • Only A and B train. If d=4096 and r=8, you learn ~65K params instead of ~16M per matrix — a ~250× reduction.
  • A is init random (Gaussian), B init zero, so ΔW = 0 at step 0 — training starts exactly at the base model, no shock.
  • α (alpha) is a scaling constant; the effective update scales by α/r. Common practice: set α = 2×r (or α = r), then tune learning rate.

Hyperparameters that matter

KnobTypicalIntuition
rank r8–16 (up to 64 for hard tasks)Higher r = more capacity + more params; diminishing returns
α (alpha)16–32 (≈ 2r)Effective LR multiplier on the adapter
target_modulesq_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj (often + MLP)Which projections get adapters; attention is the usual minimum
dropout0.05–0.1Regularize small-data runs

QLoRA — LoRA on a diet

QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) makes it fit on a single 24GB (or even smaller) GPU by quantizing the frozen base to 4-bit while training LoRA adapters in higher precision on top:

  • 4-bit NF4 (NormalFloat4) — a data type tuned for the normal distribution of weights.
  • Double quantization — quantize the quantization constants too, saving more memory.
  • Paged optimizers — offload optimizer state to CPU to survive memory spikes.
  • Result: fine-tune a 65B model on a single 48GB GPU; a 7B on a laptop-class 24GB card.
Gotcha — merge vs keep separate

You can merge an adapter back into the base (W + BA) for zero inference overhead — but then you lose hot-swapping and get one monolithic model. Keep adapters separate if you serve many tasks on one base. QLoRA adapters trained on a 4-bit base should generally be merged into an fp16 base carefully — quantization mismatch can cost a little quality.

Beyond LoRA

DoRA (weight-decomposed LoRA) splits magnitude/direction for a bit more quality; rsLoRA rescales for stability at high rank. Nice to name-drop, but plain LoRA/QLoRA is still the workhorse.

Interview soundbite

"LoRA freezes W and learns a low-rank update ΔW = B·A scaled by α/r, so you train <1% of params; B starts at zero so you begin exactly at the base. QLoRA adds a 4-bit NF4 frozen base plus double-quant and paged optimizers to fit huge models on one GPU."

lessons.title: Types of fine-tuning
lessons.lede: SFT teaches the model to imitate; preference tuning teaches it to choose; continued pre-training refills its knowledge. Know which is which.
lessons.html:
"Fine-tuning" is an umbrella. Interviewers want you to distinguish the three families and, crucially, DPO vs RLHF.

1 · Supervised fine-tuning (SFT)

The workhorse. Show the model (prompt → ideal response) pairs; it learns to imitate. Formatted with the model's chat template and trained with next-token cross-entropy. This is what "instruction tuning" is. 90% of practical fine-tuning is SFT.

2 · Preference / alignment tuning

SFT teaches one good answer; preference tuning teaches the model to prefer better over worse from (prompt, chosen, rejected) triples.

MethodHowTrade-off
RLHF (PPO)Train a reward model, then RL-optimize the policy against itPowerful, but complex, unstable, needs a reward model + RL loop
DPOSkip the reward model — a clever loss directly raises P(chosen) over P(rejected)Simple, stable, no RL; the modern default for preference tuning
ORPOCombines SFT + preference in one stage, no reference modelCheaper pipeline; newer
KTONeeds only per-example good/bad labels, not paired comparisonsEasier data collection
The DPO one-liner

DPO turns RLHF into a simple classification-style loss. No separate reward model, no PPO rollout instability — just a supervised objective over (chosen, rejected) pairs with a reference model to stay anchored. It is why alignment got accessible to small teams.

3 · Continued pre-training (domain-adaptive)

Keep doing next-token prediction on a big unlabeled domain corpus (legal, medical, code). This is the one form of fine-tuning that genuinely injects knowledge — but it is the heaviest tool, needs the most data (billions of tokens), and risks catastrophic forgetting of general ability.

The canonical post-training pipeline: (optional) continued pre-training → SFT → preference tuning.
Interview soundbite

"SFT teaches imitation from prompt→response pairs; DPO/RLHF teach preference from chosen/rejected pairs — DPO is the modern default because it drops the reward model and RL loop for a simple supervised loss; continued pre-training on raw domain text is the only variant that really injects knowledge, and it's the heaviest."

lessons.title: Data requirements & training mechanics
lessons.lede: The dirty secret: fine-tuning success is 80% data quality. Plus the failure modes that will burn you.
lessons.html:
Ask any practitioner and they will tell you the model architecture barely matters compared to the data. Fine-tuning is a data-engineering problem wearing an ML hat.

Quality > quantity (by a mile)

  • Hundreds to a few thousand high-quality examples usually beat tens of thousands of noisy ones. LIMA showed ~1,000 curated examples can align a strong base model.
  • Consistency is everything: the model imitates exactly what you show — including your mistakes, formatting drift, and contradictory labels.
  • Diversity within the task: cover edge cases, refusals, and the hard tail, not 2,000 near-duplicates.
  • Format with the base model's chat template (special tokens matter — a wrong template silently tanks results).
Failure mode 1 — catastrophic forgetting

Fine-tune too hard on a narrow task and the model forgets general abilities it had. Your JSON-extractor stops being able to hold a conversation. Mitigate with: LoRA (base frozen), low learning rate, fewer epochs, and mixing in a slice of general/instruction data.

Failure mode 2 — overfitting

Train too many epochs on too little data and it memorizes rather than generalizes. The tell: training loss keeps dropping while eval/validation loss turns up (the divergence). Fix: early stopping on a held-out set, 1–3 epochs, more/diverse data, weight decay.

Overfitting signature: train loss falls, eval loss bottoms then rises. Early-stop at the divergence.
Gotcha — you still need an eval set BEFORE you train

Hold out a representative test set the model never sees, and define your success metric first. Without it you cannot tell improvement from overfitting, and you cannot prove the fine-tune beat the prompt-only baseline.

Interview soundbite

"Fine-tuning is a data problem: a few hundred to a few thousand clean, consistent, diverse examples in the right chat template beat tens of thousands of noisy ones. Watch for catastrophic forgetting and overfitting — the tell is eval loss rising while train loss falls — and hold out an eval set before you start."

lessons.title: Measuring lift: did it actually help?
lessons.lede: The question that ends most hand-wavy answers: "how do you know your fine-tune / RAG change was better?"
lessons.html:
Any senior interview drills into evaluation, because "it feels better" is how teams ship regressions. The discipline is the same whether you changed a prompt, a retriever, or the weights: define a metric, hold out data, compare against a baseline, guard against regressions.

The non-negotiables

  • Held-out / golden set: representative examples the system never trained or was tuned on. Freeze it. This is your ruler.
  • A baseline: always compare the change to the prior approach (prompt-only, or the previous model). A fine-tune that merely matches a good prompt is not worth the ops burden.
  • Regression testing: re-run the golden set on every change so you catch the thing you accidentally broke.

How to score, per approach

Signal typeMethodNotes
Exact/structured (classification, extraction, JSON)Accuracy, F1, exact-match, schema-valid rateCheap, deterministic — use wherever possible
Open-ended generationLLM-as-judge with a rubric; pairwise A/BScalable but biased — calibrate and spot-check
RAG-specificRetrieval recall@k, faithfulness/groundedness, answer relevance (RAGAS)Separate retrieval quality from generation quality
Ground truth from usersThumbs up/down, task completion, escalation rateBest signal; laggy and noisy
Gotcha — LLM-as-judge is biased

Judge models favor longer, more confident, first-listed answers and can be gamed. Mitigate: randomize option order, use a rubric, require citations, and periodically check the judge against human labels. Never let the judge be the only gate for a high-stakes ship.

War story — the leaked test set

A team's fine-tune scored 98% offline and flopped in prod. Cause: eval examples had leaked into the training data (dedup bug), so the model had memorized the test. Always dedup train vs eval, and keep a truly untouched holdout. Contamination is the silent killer of eval.

Interview soundbite

"You prove a change with a frozen golden set, a real baseline, and regression tests — exact-match/F1 where you can, LLM-as-judge with a rubric where you can't, and for RAG you measure retrieval and generation separately. Watch for judge bias and train/test contamination."

lessons.title: Serving: multi-LoRA & deployment
lessons.lede: Training is half the job. Serving many fine-tuned variants efficiently is where LoRA quietly wins again.
lessons.html:
Once you have adapters, how do you serve them? This is where PEFT pays a second dividend and where interviewers separate the "trained a notebook once" crowd from people who ran it in production.

The multi-LoRA superpower

Because a LoRA adapter is a few MB and the base is frozen, you can host one base model in GPU memory and hot-swap hundreds of adapters on top — even batching requests for different adapters together. One 13B base can serve per-customer, per-task fine-tunes without loading N full models.

Multi-LoRA serving: one base in VRAM, many swappable/mergeable adapters — even mixed in a single batch.

Real serving stacks

ToolWhat it gives you
vLLMPagedAttention, high-throughput serving, built-in multi-LoRA support
LoRAX / S-LoRA / PunicaDynamic loading + batched inference across many adapters on one GPU
TGI (HF)Production text-generation server with adapter support
Hosted FT APIsOpenAI/Google/Cohere serve your adapter behind their endpoint — zero infra
Merge vs keep-separate (serving edition)

Merge the adapter into the base ⇒ zero per-token adapter overhead, but one model per task and no batching across tasks. Keep separate ⇒ tiny runtime cost, but one base serves many tasks and you hot-swap in ms. Multi-tenant SaaS almost always keeps them separate.

Gotcha — cold starts & quantized serving

Loading a big base is slow; keep it warm. And a model fine-tuned in fp16 then quantized to 4/8-bit for cheaper serving can lose a little quality — evaluate the quantized artifact, not just the training checkpoint.

Interview soundbite

"LoRA's second win is serving: one frozen base in VRAM plus many MB-sized adapters, hot-swapped and even batched across tasks with vLLM or LoRAX. Merge for zero overhead on a single task; keep adapters separate for multi-tenant serving — and always eval the quantized artifact you actually ship."

lessons.title: Cost & operational tradeoffs
lessons.lede: Follow the money: fine-tuning is CapEx, prompting is OpEx, and there is a break-even volume where each wins.
lessons.html:
The cleanest way to reason about which approach to use is CapEx vs OpEx. Fine-tuning is a big up-front investment (CapEx) that lowers per-call cost; prompting/RAG is pay-as-you-go (OpEx) with near-zero setup.

PromptingRAGFine-tuning
Up-front cost~0Low–med (index + pipeline)High (data + GPUs + eval)
Per-call costHigh (big prompts re-billed)Med (retrieved tokens)Low (short prompts)
Iteration speedSecondsMinutesHours–days
FreshnessPer-callInstant (reindex)Stale until retrain
Skill/behavior changeLimitedNoneStrong
Ops burdenLowMed (retriever)High (training + serving + evals)
High fixed cost + low marginal cost (fine-tuning) beats low fixed + high marginal (prompting) past a break-even volume.
The break-even mental model

Fine-tuning wins when you have high, stable call volume over a stable task — the CapEx amortizes and the cheaper short prompts dominate. Below break-even, or when the task keeps changing, prompting/RAG's zero setup wins. Prompt caching pushes the break-even further out by lowering prompting's marginal cost.

Gotcha — count the hidden costs

Fine-tuning's true cost is not the GPU hours; it is the eval harness, data pipeline, and the retrain-on-every-drift treadmill. A fine-tune that needs monthly refreshes because the domain moves can cost more than a well-cached prompt. Total cost of ownership, not sticker price.

Interview soundbite

"Fine-tuning is CapEx (big up-front, cheap per call); prompting/RAG is OpEx (zero setup, pricey per call). There's a break-even call volume — fine-tuning pays off at high, stable volume on a stable task. Count TCO: data pipeline, eval harness, and retrain treadmill, not just GPU hours."

lessons.title: Combining: RAFT, hybrid stacks, distillation
lessons.lede: The pros do not pick one. Production systems fine-tune for form, RAG for facts, and prompt for control — all at once.
lessons.html:
The three axes are orthogonal, so the strongest systems combine them. The senior move in an interview is to refuse the false choice: "it's not fine-tuning vs RAG — I'd fine-tune the behavior and retrieve the facts."

Fine-tune for FORM

Bake in the persona, the strict output schema, the domain tone, the tool-calling style. Stable, so it belongs in weights.

format & styleskills

RAG for FACTS

Retrieve the live docs, policies, and per-user data. Fresh and citable, so it belongs in the retriever.

knowledgecitations

RAFT — teaching a model to do RAG well

RAFT (Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning) fine-tunes a model on training examples that include a gold document plus distractor documents, teaching it to cite the relevant one and ignore the noise. It is "open-book exam prep": the model learns the skill of using retrieved context, which you then feed at runtime via RAG.

Distillation — bottling a big model's behavior

Distillation: use a strong teacher (GPT-4-class) to generate high-quality outputs, then SFT a smaller, cheaper student on them. You get most of the teacher's behavior at a fraction of the serving cost/latency — the dominant way teams produce small task-specialized models.

Gotcha — distillation ToS & drift

Some providers' terms restrict using their outputs to train competing models — check the license. Also, a distilled student inherits the teacher's biases and mistakes, and only covers the distribution you sampled; it will not generalize past it.

Canonical production stack

Open base → (optional) continued pre-training for domain → SFT for task/format → DPO for alignment → served with RAG for live facts → wrapped in a controlled prompt with structured outputs. All three axes, working together.

Interview soundbite

"Don't pick one — fine-tune for form, retrieve for facts, prompt for control. RAFT fine-tunes a model to use retrieved context (gold + distractors) so it cites the right doc; distillation SFTs a small student on a big teacher's outputs to bottle behavior cheaply."

lessons.title: The decision framework
lessons.lede: A repeatable flowchart you can literally draw on the whiteboard when asked "how would you approach this?"
lessons.html:
When an interviewer describes a use case and asks how you'd build it, do not guess — walk the tree out loud. Start cheap, escalate only when a real requirement forces it.

Prompt → RAG (knowledge gap) → Fine-tune (behavior gap). Combine as needed; never start at the bottom.

The escalation ladder in words

  1. Prompt engineering. Zero-shot → few-shot → CoT → structured outputs. Cache the preamble. 90% of problems stop here.
  2. Add RAG if the failure is missing/stale/private knowledge. Fix retrieval before anything else.
  3. Fine-tune if the failure is behavior/format/skill, prompting has plateaued, and you have data + volume + an eval harness.
  4. Combine: fine-tune behavior + RAG facts + controlled prompt. This is where mature systems land.
Gotcha — never lead with fine-tuning

Jumping straight to fine-tuning is the most common junior mistake. It's the slowest, priciest, least reversible knob. If you can't articulate why prompting and RAG are insufficient, you're not ready to train.

Interview soundbite

"Walk the ladder: prompt first, add RAG when the gap is knowledge, fine-tune only when the gap is behavior and prompting has plateaued — then combine. Start cheap and reversible; escalate to weights only when a concrete requirement forces it."

lessons.title: When fine-tuning is the RIGHT (and wrong) call
lessons.lede: A crisp yes/no checklist so you can defend the decision instead of hand-waving.
lessons.html:
Fine-tuning is a real tool with real wins — the skill is knowing its lane. Here are the signals, both directions.

✅ Fine-tune WHEN

  • You need a consistent output format/schema prompting can't reliably enforce.
  • You need a specific tone/persona/domain style at scale.
  • A skill the base lacks (niche language, specialized format, tool-call style).
  • Shorter prompts matter — bake in instructions to cut per-call tokens/latency.
  • High, stable volume so CapEx amortizes; the task isn't churning weekly.
  • You want to distill a big model into a cheaper small one.
  • You have quality labeled data + an eval harness.
behavior, form, skill, scale

❌ Do NOT fine-tune WHEN

  • You just need the model to know facts → that's RAG.
  • The knowledge changes often → retrain treadmill; use RAG.
  • You lack quality data (a few hundred good examples minimum).
  • You haven't exhausted prompting + few-shot + caching yet.
  • Low volume — you'll never hit break-even.
  • You need citations / provenance / per-user ACLs → RAG.
  • You can't measure whether it helped.
facts, churn, no data, no eval
War story — the format that a prompt already nailed

A team spent two weeks fine-tuning for JSON output. A reviewer pointed out the provider had shipped Structured Outputs / JSON mode that guarantees valid schema for free. The fine-tune was deleted. Always check whether a platform feature (structured outputs, function calling, prompt caching) solves it before training.

Interview soundbite

"Fine-tune for stable behavior/format/skill at high volume when you have clean data and an eval harness. Don't fine-tune for facts, fast-changing knowledge, citations, low volume, or before exhausting prompting — and check platform features like structured outputs first."

lessons.title: Interview cheat-sheet & rapid-fire Q&A
lessons.lede: Everything compressed into repeatable one-liners. Read this the morning of the interview.
lessons.html:
The 10-second frame

Prompting = instructions. RAG = knowledge. Fine-tuning = behavior. First two live in the context window (frozen weights, runtime); the third lives in the weights (a training step). Start cheap and reversible; escalate only when forced.

Rapid-fire Q&A

QuestionCrisp answer
Fine-tune to add company facts?No — use RAG. Weights are a lossy, stale, un-citable database.
What does fine-tuning do well?Behavior, format, tone, persona, skills — not fact lookup.
LoRA in one line?Freeze W, learn low-rank ΔW = B·A scaled by α/r; train <1% of params.
Why does B init to zero?So ΔW = 0 at step 0 — training starts exactly at the base model.
QLoRA additions?4-bit NF4 frozen base + double quantization + paged optimizers.
DPO vs RLHF?DPO drops the reward model + RL loop for a simple supervised loss on (chosen, rejected).
SFT vs continued pre-training?SFT = imitate prompt→response; continued pretrain = next-token on raw domain text (injects knowledge).
Overfitting tell?Train loss ↓ while eval loss ↑ — early-stop at the divergence.
Catastrophic forgetting fix?LoRA, low LR, few epochs, mix in general data.
How much data?Hundreds–thousands of clean, consistent, diverse examples; quality > quantity.
Does 1M-token context kill RAG?No — cost, latency, freshness, ACLs, 'lost in the middle' still favor retrieval at scale.
Escape the token tax without training?Prompt caching — cache the stable preamble.
Serve many fine-tunes cheaply?Multi-LoRA: one frozen base + many MB adapters (vLLM / LoRAX), hot-swapped.
Merge vs keep adapter separate?Merge = zero overhead, one task; separate = multi-tenant, hot-swap.
RAFT?Fine-tune with gold + distractor docs so the model learns to use retrieval.
Distillation?SFT a small student on a strong teacher's outputs to bottle behavior cheaply.
Prove a change helped?Frozen golden set + baseline + regression tests; watch judge bias & contamination.
Right order to try things?Prompt → RAG (knowledge gap) → fine-tune (behavior gap) → combine.
Name-drop bank (shows range)

Fine-tuning: HF PEFT/TRL, Axolotl, Unsloth, LoRA/QLoRA/DoRA, DPO/ORPO/KTO. RAG: LlamaIndex, LangChain, Pinecone/Weaviate/Qdrant/pgvector, Cohere Rerank, RAGAS. Serving: vLLM, LoRAX, S-LoRA, TGI. Prompting: Structured Outputs, function calling, prompt caching, ReAct.

The three traps to never fall into

1) "Fine-tune on our docs" for facts (use RAG). 2) Leading with fine-tuning before exhausting prompting/RAG. 3) Claiming a win with no held-out eval and no baseline.

Interview soundbite (the closer)

"Prompting changes instructions, RAG changes knowledge, fine-tuning changes behavior. Start with prompting, add RAG for a knowledge gap, fine-tune only for a behavior gap once prompting plateaus and you have data + eval — then combine all three. Facts go in the retriever, behavior goes in the weights."

quizzes.question: A team wants their support bot to answer questions about company policies that are updated weekly. Which approach is the best primary fit?

options.text: Fine-tune the model weekly on the latest policy documents
Use RAG to retrieve the current policies at query time
Continued pre-training on the policy corpus
Put all policies in a huge static system prompt

quizzes.explain: This is a knowledge problem with frequently-changing facts that need citations — RAG updates instantly by reindexing, while fine-tuning bakes stale facts into weights and forces a retrain treadmill.

quizzes.question: In LoRA, why is the B matrix initialized to zero while A is random?

options.text: To make the adapter smaller in memory
So the initial update ΔW = B·A is zero, starting training exactly at the base model
Because zero-init matrices train faster
To prevent the base weights from receiving gradients

quizzes.explain: With B=0, the product B·A is zero at step 0, so the effective weights equal the frozen base — training begins from the original model with no disruptive shock, then the adapter learns from there.

quizzes.question: What is the core innovation of QLoRA over standard LoRA?

options.text: It trains all model weights instead of just an adapter
It quantizes the frozen base model to 4-bit (NF4) with double quantization and paged optimizers
It replaces the low-rank decomposition with full-rank updates
It removes the need for any training data

quizzes.explain: QLoRA keeps the base frozen but stores it in 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4) with double quantization and paged optimizers, drastically cutting memory so large models fine-tune on a single GPU.

quizzes.question: Which statement best captures the difference between DPO and RLHF (PPO)?

options.text: DPO requires a separate reward model and an RL loop; RLHF does not
DPO optimizes preferences with a simple supervised loss on (chosen, rejected) pairs, dropping the reward model and RL loop
DPO injects new factual knowledge while RLHF only changes style
They are identical; DPO is just a rebrand of PPO

quizzes.explain: DPO reformulates preference optimization as a direct classification-style loss over chosen/rejected pairs, eliminating the separate reward model and unstable PPO rollout that classic RLHF requires.

quizzes.question: You fine-tune a model and notice training loss keeps dropping while validation loss starts rising. What is happening and what should you do?

options.text: Underfitting — train for many more epochs
Overfitting — apply early stopping, reduce epochs, or add more/diverse data
Catastrophic forgetting — switch to full fine-tuning
Normal behavior — ship it

quizzes.explain: Diverging train and validation loss is the classic overfitting signature: the model memorizes the training set instead of generalizing. Early-stop at the divergence and add data or regularization.

quizzes.question: Does a 1M-token context window make RAG obsolete?

options.text: Yes — you can just paste the entire knowledge base into the prompt
No — cost, latency, freshness, access control, and degraded recall in the middle still favor retrieval at scale
Yes, but only for models under 7B parameters
No, because long context windows cannot process documents at all

quizzes.explain: Stuffing everything in context re-bills the full window every call, adds latency, has no ACLs or freshness, and suffers "lost in the middle" degradation. Long context and RAG are complementary, not substitutes.

quizzes.question: Which technique lets you escape the per-call "token tax" of a large stable system prompt WITHOUT fine-tuning?

options.text: Chain-of-thought prompting
Prompt caching of the stable prefix
Quantizing the model to 4-bit
Increasing the temperature

quizzes.explain: Prompt caching stores a stable prompt prefix server-side so repeat calls pay a small fraction for a cache read, lowering the marginal cost of prompting and pushing the fine-tuning break-even further out.

quizzes.question: What is the main serving advantage of LoRA adapters in a multi-tenant deployment?

options.text: Each tenant requires a full copy of the model in GPU memory
One frozen base stays in memory while many small adapters are hot-swapped (and even batched) on top
Adapters eliminate the need for a base model entirely
LoRA adapters cannot be served in production

quizzes.explain: Because an adapter is a few MB and the base is frozen, systems like vLLM and LoRAX keep one base in VRAM and dynamically load/batch hundreds of adapters, avoiding N full model copies.

quizzes.question: What does RAFT (Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning) specifically train a model to do?

options.text: Memorize the entire document corpus so retrieval is unnecessary
Use retrieved context well — cite the gold document and ignore distractor documents
Compress a large teacher model into a small student
Replace the reward model in RLHF

quizzes.explain: RAFT fine-tunes on examples containing a gold document plus distractors, teaching the model the skill of grounding its answer in the relevant retrieved chunk — "open-book exam prep" that pairs with RAG at runtime.

quizzes.question: You want a fine-tune to reliably emit a fixed JSON schema. What should you check FIRST before training?

options.text: Whether the platform offers Structured Outputs / JSON mode / function calling that guarantees the schema for free
Whether you can rent enough GPUs for full fine-tuning
Whether the model supports a 1M-token context window
Whether you can quantize the model to 4-bit

quizzes.explain: Structured Outputs / JSON mode / constrained decoding already guarantee valid schemas without any training. Always check for a platform feature that solves the problem before spending time and money fine-tuning.

lessons.title: Why eval & observability is the whole game
lessons.lede: Shipping an LLM feature is easy. Knowing whether it is good, staying good, and getting better is the hard part — and it is an engineering discipline, not a vibe.
lessons.html:
Anyone can wire a prompt to an API and get a demo working in an afternoon. The thing that separates a toy from a product is a brutal question: is it actually good, and how would you even know? Traditional software has a binary oracle — the test passes or it fails. LLM systems have no such oracle. The same input can produce different outputs, "correct" is fuzzy, and quality silently rots as models, prompts, and data drift underneath you.

Eval answers "how good is it?" — measuring output quality against a bar. Observability answers "what is it actually doing in production?" — tracing every request so you can debug, attribute cost, and catch regressions. You need both: eval without observability is theater; observability without eval is a firehose of data with no verdict.

The eval-driven development (EDD) loop: every change is gated by evals, and production observations become tomorrow's test cases.

The mental model: treat quality like a metric you can move

Great teams run eval-driven development — the LLM analog of TDD. Before you tweak a prompt, you have a test set and a score. After the tweak, you re-score. If the number went down, you don't ship, no matter how good the change "felt". This turns arguing about prompts (subjective, endless) into moving a number (objective, fast).

War story: the "obvious improvement" that wasn't
A team added a polite preamble to their support-bot prompt. It read better to everyone on the team. Their eval set showed a 7% drop in answer-correctness because the preamble pushed the retrieved context further from the question and the model started ignoring it. Without the eval set, they'd have shipped a regression and blamed it on "the model getting worse".

What you'll be able to do after this course

  • Design an offline eval harness with the right metrics for the task (not just BLEU on everything).
  • Use LLM-as-judge without fooling yourself — knowing its biases and how to calibrate it.
  • Reason about statistical significance so you don't ship noise as signal.
  • Instrument production with traces/spans, run online evals, and build guardrails.
  • Catch drift and regressions before your users (and your CEO) do.
Interview soundbite
"Eval measures how good the output is; observability shows what the system actually did. Eval-driven development gates every change on an offline score, and production traces become new test cases — that closed loop is what keeps an LLM product from silently rotting."

lessons.title: Why evaluating LLMs is genuinely hard
lessons.lede: It is not that we are lazy — the medium fights back. Non-determinism, fuzzy correctness, and open-ended outputs break every assumption from classic testing.
lessons.html:
If you come from classic software testing, your instincts will betray you here. Let's enumerate exactly why, because interviewers love this question — it separates people who've shipped LLM systems from people who've read about them.

DifficultyWhat breaksConsequence
No single correct answer"Summarize this" has infinitely many good outputsExact-match assertions are useless; you need semantic or judged scoring
Non-determinismSame input → different output, even at temperature 0A test can pass then fail with zero code changes
Fuzzy correctnessOutputs are "80% right", partially hallucinatedBinary pass/fail hides most of the signal
Multi-dimensional qualityCorrect but rude, accurate but verbose, safe but uselessOne score can't capture it — you need a rubric of dimensions
Expensive ground truthLabeling needs domain experts and timeTest sets stay small; every example is precious
Moving targetProviders silently update models; your data shiftsYesterday's passing eval fails today for external reasons

The temperature=0 myth

Engineers assume temperature=0 gives determinism. It usually doesn't, for reasons below the API surface:

  • Floating-point non-associativity: GPU kernels sum in nondeterministic order; (a+b)+c != a+(b+c) at the bit level. Tiny differences flip the argmax on near-tied tokens.
  • MoE routing: mixture-of-experts models (GPT-4-class, Mixtral) route tokens to experts based on batch composition — who else is in your batch changes your output.
  • Backend changes: the provider swaps hardware, quantization, or kernels without telling you.
Gotcha: chasing bit-for-bit determinism is a trap
Don't waste weeks trying to make outputs identical. Accept variance and measure it: run each eval case 3–5 times, report mean and spread. If your metric's noise band is wider than the improvement you're claiming, you haven't improved anything — you've observed sampling noise.

Correctness is a distribution, not a point

The right frame: an LLM defines a probability distribution over outputs. A single call is one sample. Evaluating on one sample is like judging a dice as "a 4". You must sample repeatedly and reason about the distribution — which is exactly why statistical rigor (a later lesson) matters so much.

Interview soundbite
"LLM eval is hard because there's no oracle: outputs are non-deterministic even at temp 0 (FP non-associativity, MoE batch routing), correctness is fuzzy and multi-dimensional, ground truth is expensive, and the model itself is a moving target. So you measure distributions, not points."

lessons.title: The evaluation taxonomy: a map you can navigate
lessons.lede: Three axes organize every LLM eval technique. Learn the map once and you can place any tool, metric, or interview question instantly.
lessons.html:
The eval landscape looks chaotic until you see the three axes that structure it. Any technique — BLEU, RAGAS, LLM-as-judge, a thumbs-up button — is just a coordinate in this space.

Three axes place every eval method. Naming the coordinates is half of sounding senior in an interview.

Axis 1 — Offline vs Online

Offline runs before shipping, on a fixed test set, in CI. Fast, cheap, repeatable, but only as good as your test set's coverage. Online runs on live traffic — real distribution, real stakes, but noisy and you can only measure what users reveal.

Axis 2 — Reference-based vs Reference-free

Reference-based compares output to a known-good answer (needs labels; enables exact-match, BLEU, ROUGE, semantic similarity). Reference-free judges the output on its own merits or against its inputs (LLM-as-judge, RAGAS faithfulness, toxicity classifiers) — no labels needed, scales to production.

Axis 3 — Component vs System

A RAG pipeline has a retriever, a reranker, and a generator. Component evals isolate each (retriever recall@k, generator faithfulness) so you know where it broke. System evals judge the end-to-end answer the user sees. You need both: system tells you that it's broken, component tells you why.

Rule of thumb
Debug with component evals, decide-to-ship with system evals. If your end-to-end score drops, component evals are your bisect tool.
Interview soundbite
"Three axes: when (offline vs online), ground truth (reference-based vs reference-free), and scope (component vs system). Every metric is a point in that cube — say the coordinates and you sound like you've built the whole pipeline."

lessons.title: Deterministic metrics: cheap, fast, and often wrong
lessons.lede: String-overlap and embedding metrics cost nothing and run in milliseconds. Know exactly where they shine and where they lie to you.
lessons.html:
Before reaching for an expensive LLM judge, ask: can a deterministic metric do the job? These are free, instant, and perfectly repeatable — ideal for CI. The catch is they measure surface form, not meaning.

The toolbox, from strict to fuzzy

MetricMeasuresGood forFails on
Exact matchByte-identical outputClassification, structured extraction, math answersAny open-ended text
Regex / assertionsPattern presence/absenceFormat checks, forbidden-word bans, must-contain-XSemantic correctness
JSON schema validationStructural validityTool-calling, function args, API responsesWhether the values are right
BLEU / ROUGEn-gram overlap with referenceTranslation, summarization (rough signal)Paraphrases, synonyms — punishes correct rewordings
Embedding similarityCosine of sentence vectorsSemantic "is this close to the reference"Negation, precise facts, numbers ("$5" vs "$500")
Why BLEU/ROUGE lie
BLEU rewards n-gram overlap. "The cat sat on the mat" vs "A feline rested upon the rug" means the same thing and scores near zero. Meanwhile "The mat sat on the cat" — nonsense — scores high because the words overlap. Never gate a summarization or chat product on BLEU alone.

Embedding similarity: the reasonable middle ground

Encode output and reference with a sentence model (e.g. all-MiniLM-L6-v2 from sentence-transformers, 384-dim, ~fast on CPU) and take cosine similarity. It captures paraphrase. But it has sharp edges:

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util
m = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')
a = m.encode('The refund will arrive in 3 business days')
b = m.encode('You will NOT receive a refund')
print(util.cos_sim(a, b))  # ~0.6 — dangerously high despite opposite meaning

Embeddings smear over negation and precise facts. A "3 days" vs "30 days" answer can look 95% similar while being catastrophically wrong for the user.

Rule of thumb
Use deterministic metrics as guardrails and fast filters, not as your quality verdict. Exact-match/schema for anything structured; embedding similarity as a cheap regression tripwire; escalate the fuzzy stuff to an LLM judge or human.

Layered scoring

Run the cheap gate first, escalate only failures. Schema-valid? → embedding-close to a golden? → if borderline, send to LLM judge. Cuts judge cost by 5–10x on a passing test set.

use when high volume + cost mattersavoid when every case needs nuance
Interview soundbite
"Deterministic metrics are free and repeatable but measure form, not meaning. BLEU punishes paraphrase and rewards word-salad; embedding similarity misses negation and exact facts. I use them as fast CI gates and escalate ambiguous cases to a judge."

lessons.title: LLM-as-a-judge: scalable, powerful, and biased
lessons.lede: Using a strong model to grade outputs is the workhorse of modern eval. It also has systematic biases that will fool you if you do not defend against them.
lessons.html:
The breakthrough idea: use a capable model (GPT-4-class, Claude) to score outputs against a rubric. It approximates human judgment at a fraction of the cost and scales to thousands of cases. Research (MT-Bench, Zheng et al. 2023) found strong judges agree with human preferences ~80%+ of the time — roughly the rate humans agree with each other. But a judge is a model, and models have biases.

Three modes of judging

ModePromptBest for
Direct scoring"Rate this answer 1–5 on faithfulness"Absolute quality, monitoring dashboards
Pairwise"Is answer A or B better?"Comparing two prompts/models; more reliable than absolute scores
Reference-guided"Given this gold answer, grade the candidate"When you have labels and want grounded grading
Rule of thumb
Humans (and judges) are far better at comparing than at assigning absolute scores. Prefer pairwise when you can; it has lower variance and clearer signal.

The bias rogues' gallery

BiasWhat happensMitigation
Position biasPrefers whichever answer is shown first (or second)Run both orders, average; only count if verdict is consistent
Verbosity biasLonger answers score higher regardless of qualityAdd "ignore length" to rubric; normalize; penalize fluff explicitly
Self-preferenceJudge favors outputs from its own model familyUse a different model family as judge; or a panel
SycophancyAgrees with assertions embedded in the promptDon't leak the "expected" answer; neutral framing
Format biasMarkdown/bullets score higher than plain proseControl for format; strip formatting before judging if irrelevant

Controlling position bias (the code you should know)

def pairwise(judge, question, a, b):
    v1 = judge(question, first=a, second=b)   # A then B
    v2 = judge(question, first=b, second=a)   # B then A (swapped)
